title: India's Three-Stage Nuclear Programme and MBRAPP
focus: Stages of India's nuclear program and the Mahi Banswara Rajasthan Atomic Power Project.
rationale: Covers the foundational 3-stage nuclear program of India and a recent specific project (MBRAPP) utilizing PHWR technology.
sectionExcerpt: India's three-stage Nuclear Programme Stage 1: Heavy water reactors (PHWR) fuelled by natural Uranium produce plutonium. Stage 2: Fuelled by a mix of Plutonium and natural Uranium. With sufficient stocks, thorium is introduced to convert it into U-233. Stage 3: Mix of Thorium and Uranium fuels the Reactors. Thorium transmutes to U 233, which powers the reactor. Mahi Banswara Rajasthan Atomic Power Project (MBRAPP) Capacity: 4 x 700 MWe PHWR. Development: It will be developed by Anushakti Vidhyut Nigam (ASHVINI), a joint venture between Nuclear Power Corporation India Limited (NPCIL) and National Thermal Power Corporation Limited (NTPC). Fleet Mode: The project is part of India's fleet mode initiative.
evidenceBullets: Stage 1 of India's nuclear program uses Natural Uranium in PHWRs to produce 10 GWe electricity, Depleted Uranium, and Plutonium.
Stage 2 uses Plutonium and Natural Uranium in Fast Breeders, introducing Thorium to convert it to U-233.
Stage 3 uses a mix of Thorium and Uranium, where Thorium transmutes to U-233 to power the reactor.
MBRAPP is a 4 x 700 MWe PHWR project developed by ASHVINI, a joint venture between NPCIL and NTPC.
MBRAPP is part of India's fleet mode initiative to build ten identical 700 MW reactors under uniform design.

title: Uranium Enrichment
focus: The process, methods, and classification of enriched uranium.
rationale: A distinct scientific concept detailing how uranium is enriched and the specific percentage thresholds for civilian versus military use.
sectionExcerpt: Enrichment of uranium Definition: To enrich uranium means taking the naturally found element and increasing the proportion of uranium-235 while removing uranium-238. Method: Mainly done using centrifuges. Enrichment Levels & Uses: (3-5%) U-235: Civilian nuclear power reactors. (>20%) U-235: Highly enriched uranium (HEU), potential for weapons. (~90%) U-235: Weapons-grade, compact and efficient for nuclear arms.
evidenceBullets: Uranium enrichment increases the proportion of U-235 while removing U-238, primarily using centrifuges.
Civilian nuclear power reactors typically use uranium enriched to 3-5% U-235.
Highly enriched uranium (HEU) contains greater than 20% U-235 and has potential for weapons.
Weapons-grade uranium is enriched to approximately 90% U-235.

title: Thorium Molten Salt Reactor (TMSR) and Thorium Reserves
focus: TMSR technology, characteristics of Thorium fuel, and India's Thorium reserves.
rationale: Groups the TMSR technology with the properties of Thorium fuel and India's specific reserves, forming a comprehensive block on Thorium-based nuclear energy.
sectionExcerpt: China successfully achieved the first-ever thorium to uranium fuel conversion in a Thorium Molten Salt Reactor (TMSR). It is fourth-generation advanced nuclear energy system that uses high-temperature molten salt as a coolant. Passive Safety Features: If an MSR is too hot, a salt plug melts, stopping the reaction. Thorium has to be converted to Uranium-233 in a reactor before it can be used as fuel. Thorium-232 is the only naturally occurring isotope of thorium. India has the world's largest reserves of thorium. Kerala and Odisha have rich reserves of monazite, which contains about 8 - 10% thorium.
evidenceBullets: TMSR is a 4th-generation nuclear system using high-temperature molten salt as a coolant, with China operating the only current TMSR.
TMSRs feature passive safety mechanisms, such as a salt plug that melts to stop the reaction if the reactor overheats.
Thorium-232 is the only naturally occurring isotope of thorium and must be converted to Uranium-233 to be used as fuel.
Thorium is three times more abundant than uranium in nature, but its utilization is restricted by high extraction costs.
India holds the world's largest thorium reserves, primarily found in monazite sands in Kerala, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, West Bengal, and Jharkhand.
India is developing the Advanced Heavy Water Reactor (AHWR) and Indian Molten Salt Breeder Reactor (IMSBR) for the thorium fuel cycle.

title: Lithium-Ion Batteries and Black Mass Recovery
focus: Working mechanism, benefits of Li-ion batteries, and black mass recycling technology.
rationale: A separate alternative energy topic focusing on battery storage technology and the recycling of critical minerals.
sectionExcerpt: India's Largest Lithium-ion (Li-ion) Battery (LIB) manufacturing plant was inaugurated in Haryana. In it, lithium ions move between a negative electrode (graphite) and a positive electrode (Li transitional metal oxides) through a non-aqueous electrolyte during charging and discharging. Benefits: High energy density, Lightweight and Lower Toxic Heavy Metals, Excellent performance with generally no memory effect. Black Mass Recovery Technology: Technology Development Board (TDB) has extended financial support for the commercialization of an indigenous battery recycling technology. Black mass is a dark, powdery substance from used batteries containing critical minerals like lithium, cobalt, and nickel.
evidenceBullets: In Li-ion batteries, lithium ions move between a graphite negative electrode and a Li transitional metal oxide positive electrode via a non-aqueous electrolyte.
Li-ion batteries offer high energy density, lightweight properties, and generally no memory effect compared to older lead-acid batteries.
Black mass is a dark, powdery substance recovered from end-of-life lithium-ion batteries.
Black mass recovery extracts critical minerals like lithium, cobalt, and nickel for refinement and reuse in clean energy.
The Technology Development Board (TDB) is supporting indigenous dual-mode (wet and dry) black mass recovery technology.
